title: The tags that say what a thing is
why.no-header: The top part is still `<div class="top">`. "Top" is a word you invented. `<header>` is a word the browser understands.
why.no-nav: The menu is still a group. There is a tag for navigation — you have known it since lesson 35.
why.no-main: There is no main content. If everything on the page is equally important, nothing is. `<main>` says: this is the point, the rest is setting.
why.no-article: The listing is a group. But it is a standalone thing — lift it out of the page and it still makes sense. That is exactly what `<article>` means.
why.no-footer: The bottom part is still a group. `<footer>` says: the content ends here.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed.
blocks.0.text: The code on the right is better than the last one — it has real headings, a real list. But the wrappers are still groups: "top", "middle", "bottom".
blocks.1.text: Those words you invented. The browser does not understand them.
blocks.3.items.0: <header> — the top part. Logo, site title, usually the menu too.
blocks.3.items.2: <main> — the main content. Exactly one per page.
blocks.3.items.3: <article> — a standalone thing. An article, a listing, a comment.
blocks.3.items.4: <section> — a section of something. Part of a whole.
blocks.3.items.5: <footer> — the bottom part. Copyright, contacts, the fine print.
blocks.4.text: There is also <aside> — something off to the side that is not part of the main thing: an advert, "similar listings", a sidebar.
blocks.6.text: Everyone gets confused here, so here is the test: lift the thing out of the page and put it somewhere else. Does it make sense on its own?
blocks.7.text: A bike listing — yes. An article — yes. A comment — yes. Those are <article>.
blocks.8.text: An "About us" section on its own? It means nothing. That is a <section>.
blocks.10.text: <main> is special: only one on the entire page. It says "this is the point, the rest is setting".
blocks.11.text: The menu is not in it. The footer is not in it. The logo is not in it. They repeat on every page — they are not the point of THIS one.
blocks.12.text: Notice what you achieve: a blind person gets a "skip to main content" button. One press — and they have skipped the menu they have already heard fifty times.
blocks.14.text: Run it before and after. No difference. None at all.
blocks.15.text: This is the third thing in this course that is invisible and matters. The first was <title>. The second was alt. Now this.
blocks.16.text: You are starting to see the pattern: HTML is full of things that are not for the eye.
blocks.17.text: I do not write HTML for the browser. I write it for the ones who are not looking at the screen.
blocks.18.text: And the most practical part: these tags cannot be added later. CSS changes in an hour. A style gets redone. But if you wrote the page out of groups, the meaning is not lost — it was never there.
blocks.19.text: Fix the code on the right.
blocks.20.text: And note: this is not a task about looks. You are rewriting the page for people who will never see it.
blocks.22.text: There is a test that takes thirty seconds and needs nothing at all.
blocks.23.text: Close your eyes. Read your code aloud — only the tags, none of the content.
blocks.24.text: "Header. Nav. List of two links. Main. Article. Heading two. Paragraph. Footer."
blocks.25.text: Do you hear the structure of a page? If yes — you did it.
blocks.26.text: Now try it with the old code: "Div. Div. List. Div. Div. Heading. Div."
blocks.28.text: That is exactly what someone who cannot see your screen hears. It is not an exercise — it is their daily life.
blocks.29.text: And the best part: this test needs no tools. Only ears.
